text:
Daily Reflection Tree

Three passes, one conversation: control, contribution, and the size of the frame.
Before we sort anything, what kind of day is still sitting with you?
options: A day with some traction.
A day that felt heavier than it looked.
A day full of moving parts.
A day I am still trying to read.

routing: answer=A day with some traction.|A day full of moving parts.:OPEN_R_STEADY;answer=A day that felt heavier than it looked.|A day I am still trying to read.:OPEN_R_HEAVY

text: That kind of day usually has texture worth looking at closely, not just a score to assign.
Let's start with where the day felt steerable, or not.
Some days stay foggy even after they end. Naming the weight is enough to begin.
Let's start with where the day felt steerable, or not.
When the day pushed back, which line sounded more like you?
options: I looked for the next move I could make.
I kept noticing the parts I could not change.
I adjusted as I went and kept some agency.
I was mostly reacting to forces around me.

routing: answer=I looked for the next move I could make.|I adjusted as I went and kept some agency.:A1_Q2_INT;answer=I kept noticing the parts I could not change.|I was mostly reacting to forces around me.:A1_Q2_EXT

text: Where did that agency show up most clearly?
options: I changed my approach instead of repeating it.
I asked for help or context sooner.
I chose one useful task and finished that first.
I set a boundary and stopped trying to carry everything.

text: You seem to remember the day through choices, even small ones. The detail about "{A1_Q2_INT.answer}" is often where agency becomes visible.
Let's keep that thread and shift from control to contribution.
What kept your attention on the outside pressure?
options: Other people's delays changed my whole rhythm.
The priorities kept shifting around me.
I spent energy waiting for clarity that never really came.
I was carrying the mood of the room as much as the work.

text: Days can feel smaller when so much of the weather seems elsewhere. Noticing "{A1_Q2_EXT.answer}" does not mean surrender; it shows where your attention had to keep going.
Let's keep going gently and shift from control to contribution.
As you look back, what were you most aware of trading all day?
options: What I could give to keep things moving.
What support or recognition I felt was missing.
Where I could be useful, even quietly.
How much I was putting in compared with what I was getting back.

routing: answer=What I could give to keep things moving.|Where I could be useful, even quietly.:A2_Q2_CON;answer=What support or recognition I felt was missing.|How much I was putting in compared with what I was getting back.:A2_Q2_ENT

text: What did your contribution look like in practice?
options: I made something clearer for someone else.
I picked up a loose task before it spread.
I shared effort, context, or credit.
I protected the pace by staying dependable.

text: Your memory of the day leans toward what you added, not just what you extracted from it. Contribution is often ordinary in the moment and obvious only in hindsight.
The next turn widens the frame a little.
What felt most absent or overdue?
options: Clear direction.
Recognition for work that was already happening.
Fair distribution of effort.
Timely help from the people around me.

text: When the mind keeps returning to what was missing, it is usually pointing at a real gap, not inventing one. The useful question is whether the gap defined the whole day or just one part of it.
The next turn widens the frame a little.
By the end of the day, what did the story mostly revolve around?
options: My workload, my deadlines, my headspace.
The team, the handoffs, and how others were doing.
My energy and how much stretch I had left.
The people affected downstream by our work.

routing: answer=My workload, my deadlines, my headspace.|My energy and how much stretch I had left.:A3_Q2_SELF;answer=The team, the handoffs, and how others were doing.|The people affected downstream by our work.:A3_Q2_OTHER

text: When your view stayed close to home, what was it protecting?
options: My focus.
My energy.
My sense of competence.
My ability to get through the day at all.

text: A narrow frame is sometimes what lets a day remain manageable. It can be a form of protection, not just self-absorption.
One last look, pulling the threads together.
When your view widened, who was most present in it?
options: Teammates who depended on my part.
Someone who was blocked or overloaded.
Customers or users on the receiving end.
The wider group rhythm, not just my own lane.

text: Your account of the day includes more than your own perimeter. That wider lens can change how effort feels, because it places the day inside a shared system.
One last look, pulling the threads together.
End of reflection. The point was not to fix the day, only to see its shape a little more clearly.
